unit.title: Early Republic Study Tool
unit.subtitle: Get ready to ace your test! Learn vocabulary, practice questions, and track your progress.
unit.essentialQuestion: How did the first leaders shape the country we live in today?

vocabulary.definition: The formal ceremony where a president takes the oath of office
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: It's like a big swearing-in party where the new president promises to do a good job. Think of it like the first day at a new school, but for running the whole country.
vocabulary.example: George Washington's inauguration in 1789 set the first precedent for how presidents take office
vocabulary.typingSnippet: An inauguration is the formal ceremony where a president takes the oath of office and officially begins their term. Washington's inauguration in 1789 was the very first, setting the standard for every president since. It established the tradition of taking the oath of office that every president has followed since.

vocabulary.definition: A group of advisors appointed by the President to lead executive departments
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Imagine the president picks a team of experts to help make big decisions — one for money, one for talking to other countries, one for the military. That's the Cabinet.
vocabulary.example: Washington's Cabinet included Thomas Jefferson (State), Alexander Hamilton (Treasury), and Henry Knox (War)
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Cabinet is a group of advisors chosen by the president to lead the executive departments. Washington created the first Cabinet with Jefferson, Hamilton, and Knox. This system of expert advisors helping the president make decisions continues today.

vocabulary.definition: An action or decision that becomes an example for the future
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: When you're the first to do something and everyone copies you after that. Like if you're the first kid to hold the door open, and then everyone starts doing it — you set a precedent.
vocabulary.example: Washington set the precedent of serving only two terms as president
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A precedent is an action that sets an example for others to follow. Washington created many precedents, like stepping down after two terms, that future presidents would respect for over a century. His choices shaped how the presidency works to this day.

vocabulary.definition: Someone who supported a strong federal government; led by Hamilton
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Team Hamilton! They wanted the national government to be really powerful — like having a strong principal who makes the rules for the whole school, not just each classroom.
vocabulary.example: Federalists like Hamilton wanted a strong central government and a national bank
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Federalists were led by Alexander Hamilton and believed in a strong central government. They supported a national bank, protective tariffs, and a powerful military. Their vision helped build the financial foundation of the United States.

vocabulary.definition: Someone who opposed a strong federal government; favored states' rights
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The opposite team — they thought each state should mostly run itself. Like wanting each classroom teacher to make their own rules instead of the principal deciding everything.
vocabulary.example: Anti-Federalists feared a powerful central government would threaten individual liberties
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Anti-Federalists opposed giving too much power to the central government. They believed states should hold more authority and feared that a strong federal government could become like the monarchy they had just fought to escape. Their pressure led to the Bill of Rights.

vocabulary.definition: Hamilton's proposed central bank to manage the nation's finances
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Hamilton wanted one big bank for the whole country to hold everyone's money and pay the bills. Think of it like a school treasurer who manages all the fundraiser money for every club.
vocabulary.example: Hamilton argued the National Bank was necessary to print currency and manage debt
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Hamilton proposed a National Bank to manage the country's money and pay off war debts. Jefferson argued the Constitution did not allow it, sparking a major debate over government power. Washington sided with Hamilton, and the bank was created in 1791.

vocabulary.definition: A tax on imported goods, used to raise government revenue
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: When you buy something from another country, the government adds an extra charge on it. This makes foreign stuff more expensive so people buy American-made stuff instead.
vocabulary.example: Hamilton proposed tariffs on imports to protect American manufacturing
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A tariff is a tax placed on goods imported from other countries. Hamilton pushed for tariffs to raise money for the government and to help American factories compete with cheaper foreign products. Tariffs became one of the most debated topics in early American politics.

vocabulary.definition: Executive department handling finances; first led by Alexander Hamilton
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The government's money manager. Hamilton was in charge of figuring out how to pay bills, collect taxes, and make sure the country didn't go broke.
vocabulary.example: Hamilton used the Dept. of Treasury to create plans for paying off the national debt
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Department of Treasury was created to handle the nation's money, and Alexander Hamilton was its first leader. He developed a plan to pay off war debts and build the country's credit. His financial decisions helped the young nation gain trust from other countries.

vocabulary.definition: Executive department handling foreign affairs; first led by Thomas Jefferson
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The person in charge of being friends (or not) with other countries. Jefferson's job was basically to talk to the rest of the world on America's behalf.
vocabulary.example: Jefferson led the Dept. of State, managing America's relationships with other countries
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Department of State handles the nation's foreign affairs, and Thomas Jefferson was its first leader. He managed relationships with countries like Britain and France during a very tense time. This department still oversees American diplomacy around the world today.

vocabulary.definition: Executive department handling military affairs; first led by Henry Knox
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The person in charge of the army and keeping the country safe. Henry Knox ran the military — today we call it the Department of Defense.
vocabulary.example: Henry Knox led the Dept. of War, overseeing the nation's small military
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Department of War was responsible for the nation's military, and Henry Knox was chosen as its first leader. Knox had served as a general in the Revolutionary War, so he knew the military well. This department later became what we now call the Department of Defense.

vocabulary.definition: A 1794 uprising by farmers protesting the federal tax on whiskey
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Farmers were mad about a tax on whiskey (which they made from their extra grain) and started a revolt. Washington sent the army to stop them — proving the government was serious about enforcing its rules.
vocabulary.example: Washington sent the army to crush the Whiskey Rebellion, proving the federal government could enforce its laws
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Whiskey Rebellion broke out in 1794 when farmers in western Pennsylvania refused to pay a federal tax on whiskey. President Washington personally led troops to put down the revolt. This proved that the new federal government had the power to enforce its laws.

vocabulary.definition: A 1794 agreement with Britain that removed British forts but limited US trade
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: America made a deal with Britain: 'Leave our forts alone.' But Britain still got to mess with American ships. Most people thought it was a terrible deal, but it kept the peace.
vocabulary.example: Jay's Treaty was unpopular because it didn't fully stop Britain from seizing American ships
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Jay's Treaty was a 1794 agreement with Britain that got the British to leave forts on American soil. However, it did little to stop Britain from seizing American ships and sailors. Many Americans were furious, and the treaty deepened the divide between Federalists and Democratic-Republicans.

vocabulary.definition: An incident where French agents demanded bribes before negotiating with US diplomats
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: American diplomats went to France to talk, but French officials said 'Pay us money first before we'll even have a conversation.' Americans were furious — it was basically a shakedown.
vocabulary.example: The XYZ Affair outraged Americans and led to an undeclared Quasi-War with France under President Adams
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The XYZ Affair happened when French officials demanded bribes before they would even talk to American diplomats. Americans were outraged, and the incident nearly led to a full war with France. It showed how fragile the young nation's relationships with European powers really were.

vocabulary.definition: An organized group that seeks to influence government policy by winning elections
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A team of people who agree on how the country should be run. Think of it like rival friend groups who each think they know the best way to do things.
vocabulary.example: America's first political parties — Federalists and Democratic-Republicans — formed from disagreements between Hamilton and Jefferson
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A political party is an organized group of people who share similar ideas about how government should work. The first two parties in America grew out of disagreements between Hamilton and Jefferson. Even though the Founders warned against parties, they became a lasting part of American democracy.

vocabulary.definition: Political party led by Jefferson; favored limited federal power and farmers' interests
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Team Jefferson! They thought the government should stay small and leave people alone. They were all about farmers and regular people, not big banks and businesses.
vocabulary.example: Democratic-Republicans believed the government should have limited power and support agriculture
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Democratic-Republican Party was led by Thomas Jefferson and James Madison. They believed the federal government should have limited power and that farmers were the backbone of America. This party represented the opposite vision from Hamilton's Federalists.

vocabulary.definition: Belief that the government should only do what the Constitution specifically allows
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: If the Constitution doesn't say you can do it, you can't do it. It's like following the rules of a game exactly — no making up new rules as you go.
vocabulary.example: Jefferson used strict construction to argue against the National Bank — the Constitution doesn't mention it
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Strict construction means interpreting the Constitution exactly as it is written. Jefferson believed the government could only do what the Constitution specifically allowed. He used this argument to oppose the National Bank, since the Constitution never mentioned creating one.

vocabulary.definition: Belief that the government has implied powers beyond what the Constitution explicitly states
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The Constitution doesn't have to say everything word-for-word. If something makes sense and helps the country, the government can probably do it. It's like reading between the lines.
vocabulary.example: Hamilton used loose construction to justify the National Bank under the 'Necessary and Proper' clause
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Loose construction means the Constitution gives the government implied powers beyond what is written directly. Hamilton pointed to the Necessary and Proper Clause to argue that Congress could create a national bank. This flexible reading of the Constitution expanded federal power significantly.

vocabulary.definition: A permanent military force maintained in peacetime
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: An army that's always there, even when there's no war. Some people thought this was smart for protection. Others worried it could be used to bully citizens.
vocabulary.example: Federalists favored a large standing army, while Democratic-Republicans feared it could threaten liberty
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A standing army is a permanent military force that remains active even during peacetime. Federalists wanted one for national defense, but Democratic-Republicans worried it could be used to take away people's freedoms. This debate over military power was one of the biggest disagreements between the two parties.

vocabulary.definition: A firsthand account or original document from the time period being studied
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Something made by someone who was actually there. A diary entry, a photo, a speech — it's the real deal, not someone else talking about it later.
vocabulary.example: A letter written by Thomas Jefferson in 1803 about the Louisiana Purchase is a primary source
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A primary source is a firsthand account or original document from the time being studied. Letters, diaries, photographs, and speeches are all examples. Historians rely on primary sources to understand what people actually thought and experienced during historical events.

vocabulary.definition: A strategy for evaluating sources: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Before you believe something you read, check it! Stop and think, look up who wrote it, see if other sources say the same thing, and trace it back to where it started.
vocabulary.example: Use the SIFT Method to check if a website about the Early Republic is reliable before citing it
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The SIFT Method is a four-step process for evaluating sources: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, and Trace claims back to the original. It helps you figure out whether information is trustworthy before you use it. This skill is especially important when researching online.

vocabulary.definition: An account created after an event by someone who did not directly witness it
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Something written about an event by someone who wasn't there. Your history textbook is a secondary source — the author is describing what happened, but they didn't live through it.
vocabulary.example: A textbook chapter about Washington's presidency is a secondary source
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A secondary source is an account written after an event by someone who was not there to witness it. Textbooks, documentaries, and encyclopedia articles are common examples. Secondary sources help us interpret and understand primary sources by putting them in context.

vocabulary.definition: The power of the Supreme Court to declare laws unconstitutional; established by Marbury v. Madison
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The Supreme Court can look at any law and say 'Nope, that breaks the rules of the Constitution.' It's like having a referee who can throw a flag on Congress.
vocabulary.example: Judicial review gave the Supreme Court real power to check Congress and the President
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Judicial review is the power of the Supreme Court to decide whether a law goes against the Constitution. This power was established in the landmark case Marbury v. Madison in 1803. It gave the judicial branch a real way to check the power of Congress and the president.

vocabulary.definition: The 1803 Supreme Court case that established the principle of judicial review
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: This court case is where the Supreme Court first said 'We can strike down laws that break the Constitution.' It gave the courts real power for the first time.
vocabulary.example: Chief Justice John Marshall's ruling in Marbury v. Madison created one of the most important checks and balances
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Marbury v. Madison was an 1803 Supreme Court case decided by Chief Justice John Marshall. Marshall ruled that a law passed by Congress was unconstitutional, creating the power of judicial review. This case is one of the most important in American history because it strengthened the Supreme Court forever.

vocabulary.definition: Something that violates or goes against the Constitution
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: It means 'against the rules.' If a law breaks the Constitution, it's unconstitutional and has to be thrown out.
vocabulary.example: In Marbury v. Madison, the Supreme Court declared a law unconstitutional for the first time
vocabulary.typingSnippet: When something is unconstitutional, it means it violates or goes against what the Constitution allows. The Supreme Court has the power to strike down any law it finds unconstitutional. This concept is a key part of how checks and balances protect Americans' rights.

vocabulary.definition: A successful slave revolt in Haiti (1791-1804) that influenced Napoleon's decision to sell Louisiana
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Enslaved people in Haiti fought back and won their freedom — the only successful slave revolt to create a whole new country. It also scared Napoleon into selling Louisiana to the US.
vocabulary.example: The Haitian Revolution terrified Southern slaveholders but inspired people fighting for freedom worldwide
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Haitian Revolution was a successful revolt by enslaved people in Haiti that lasted from 1791 to 1804. It was the only slave revolt in history to create an independent nation. The revolution also caused Napoleon to give up on his American empire, which led directly to the Louisiana Purchase.

vocabulary.definition: The 1803 purchase of French territory that doubled the size of the United States
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Jefferson bought a HUGE chunk of land from France for $15 million — it doubled the size of the whole country. Imagine buying half a continent for about 4 cents an acre!
vocabulary.example: Jefferson bought the Louisiana Territory from Napoleon for $15 million, even though the Constitution didn't specifically allow it
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Louisiana Purchase in 1803 doubled the size of the United States overnight. Jefferson bought the massive territory from France for just fifteen million dollars, which was an incredible deal. The purchase created a dilemma for Jefferson because the Constitution said nothing about buying new land.

vocabulary.definition: French leader who sold the Louisiana Territory to the US to fund his European wars
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A powerful French leader who needed cash for his wars in Europe. He sold the Louisiana Territory to America because he couldn't hold onto it after losing Haiti.
vocabulary.example: Napoleon gave up his American empire after the Haitian Revolution made holding the territory too costly
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Napoleon was the leader of France who sold the Louisiana Territory to the United States. He needed money to fund his wars in Europe and had lost control of Haiti after the slave revolt there. His decision to sell changed the future of America by giving the young nation room to grow westward.

vocabulary.definition: The expedition led by Lewis and Clark to explore the Louisiana Territory
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Lewis and Clark's adventure team! They hiked across the whole western half of America, drawing maps, finding new animals, and meeting Native American nations nobody in the government knew about.
vocabulary.example: The Corps of Discovery traveled from 1804 to 1806, mapping the territory and documenting plants, animals, and peoples
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Corps of Discovery was the expedition team led by Meriwether Lewis and William Clark to explore the Louisiana Territory. From 1804 to 1806, they mapped new land, recorded plants and animals, and met many Native American nations. Their journey gave Americans their first real look at the western part of the continent.

vocabulary.definition: A journey undertaken for a specific purpose, especially exploration
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A trip with a mission. Not a vacation — you're going somewhere specific to explore, discover, or accomplish something important.
vocabulary.example: Jefferson sent Lewis and Clark on an expedition to find a water route to the Pacific Ocean
vocabulary.typingSnippet: An expedition is a journey organized for a specific purpose, like exploration or scientific research. Jefferson sent Lewis and Clark on an expedition to explore the Louisiana Territory and find a route to the Pacific Ocean. Their trip took over two years and covered thousands of miles of unknown land.

vocabulary.definition: A Shoshone woman who served as interpreter and guide for the Lewis and Clark expedition
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A Shoshone woman who translated languages and showed Lewis and Clark the way through unfamiliar territory. Without her, the expedition probably would have failed.
vocabulary.example: Sacagawea helped the Corps of Discovery communicate with Native nations and navigate the western territory
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Sacagawea was a Shoshone woman who served as an interpreter and guide for Lewis and Clark's expedition. She helped the team communicate with Native American nations and navigate unfamiliar territory. Her presence also signaled to Native peoples that the expedition came in peace.

vocabulary.definition: North African pirates who attacked American ships and demanded tribute payments
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Pirates from North Africa who attacked American ships and said 'Pay us or we'll keep attacking.' Jefferson said 'No way' and sent the navy to fight them instead.
vocabulary.example: Jefferson refused to keep paying tribute to the Barbary Pirates and sent the navy to fight them instead
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Barbary Pirates were raiders from North Africa who attacked American trading ships in the Mediterranean Sea. For years, the U.S. paid them tribute money for protection, but Jefferson refused to keep paying and sent the navy instead. This conflict showed the world that America would fight to defend its interests.

vocabulary.definition: Payment made to a foreign power to prevent attacks or gain protection
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Basically protection money. 'Pay us and we won't attack you.' It's like a bully saying 'Give me your lunch money and I'll leave you alone.'
vocabulary.example: Before Jefferson, the US paid tribute to North African states to protect American merchant ships
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Tribute is a payment made to a foreign power in exchange for protection or to avoid being attacked. The United States paid tribute to the Barbary States of North Africa to keep American ships safe. Jefferson eventually decided that fighting was better than continuing to pay.

vocabulary.definition: The British practice of forcing American sailors to serve in the Royal Navy
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Britain would stop American ships and kidnap the sailors, forcing them to work on British warships. Imagine someone grabbing you off the street and making you work for them — that's impressment.
vocabulary.example: Impressment of American sailors was one of the key causes of the War of 1812
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Impressment was the British practice of capturing American sailors and forcing them to serve in the Royal Navy. Britain claimed it was only taking back deserters, but many of those seized were actually American citizens. This insult to American sovereignty became one of the main causes of the War of 1812.

vocabulary.definition: An 1807 law that banned all US trade with foreign nations to pressure Britain and France
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Jefferson said 'Fine, we won't trade with ANYONE!' to punish Britain and France. But it backfired badly — American farmers and businesses suffered the most.
vocabulary.example: The Embargo Act backfired — it hurt American merchants and farmers more than it hurt Britain or France
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Embargo Act of 1807 banned all American trade with foreign nations to punish Britain and France. Instead of hurting those countries, it devastated American farmers and merchants who depended on trade. The act was so unpopular that it was repealed, and it remains a famous example of a policy backfiring.

vocabulary.definition: A situation requiring a choice between two difficult or undesirable options
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: When you're stuck between two bad choices. Like if your two best friends both want you to hang out on the same day — no matter what you pick, someone's upset.
vocabulary.example: Jefferson faced a dilemma: buying Louisiana required loose construction, which he had always opposed
vocabulary.typingSnippet: A dilemma is a situation where you must choose between two difficult options. Jefferson faced a famous dilemma when offered the Louisiana Territory: he believed in strict construction, but the Constitution said nothing about buying land. He went ahead with the purchase anyway, showing that even strong beliefs can bend when the stakes are high.

vocabulary.definition: Members of Congress who pushed for war with Britain in 1812; led by Henry Clay
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Young politicians who were fired up and wanted to fight Britain. They were tired of being pushed around and thought a war would prove America was strong.
vocabulary.example: War Hawks like Henry Clay wanted to defend American honor and expand into British Canada
vocabulary.typingSnippet: War Hawks were young members of Congress who pushed hard for war with Britain in the early 1800s. Led by Henry Clay, they were angry about impressment and wanted to expand American territory into Canada. Their influence helped convince Congress to declare the War of 1812.

vocabulary.definition: Shawnee leader who built a confederation of Native nations to resist American expansion
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A Shawnee chief who tried to get all the Native American nations to team up and say 'Stop taking our land!' He was one of the strongest leaders fighting against American expansion.
vocabulary.example: Tecumseh tried to unite Native American nations to stop settlers from taking their land
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Tecumseh was a Shawnee leader who worked to unite Native American nations into a confederation to resist American expansion. He argued that no single tribe could sell land that belonged to all Native peoples. His alliance with Britain during the War of 1812 made him one of America's most formidable opponents.

vocabulary.definition: Kentucky congressman and War Hawk who later proposed the American System
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A Kentucky politician who wanted war with Britain AND had a big plan to make America's economy stronger afterwards. He was one of the most important voices in Congress for years.
vocabulary.example: Henry Clay pushed for the War of 1812 and later created the American System to strengthen the economy
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Henry Clay was a Kentucky congressman who became one of the most influential politicians of his era. As a War Hawk, he pushed for the War of 1812 to defend American honor. After the war, he proposed the American System, a plan to strengthen the nation's economy through tariffs, a national bank, and better roads.

vocabulary.definition: Baltimore fort whose defense during a British bombardment inspired 'The Star-Spangled Banner'
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Britain bombed this fort ALL night long, but when the sun came up, the American flag was still flying. A guy watching named Francis Scott Key was so moved he wrote our national anthem about it.
vocabulary.example: Francis Scott Key watched the bombardment of Fort McHenry and wrote the national anthem after seeing the flag still flying
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Fort McHenry was the Baltimore fort that withstood a massive British bombardment during the War of 1812. The battle lasted through the night, and when morning came, the American flag was still flying over the fort. This moment inspired Francis Scott Key to write the poem that became our national anthem.

vocabulary.definition: The 1814 peace treaty that ended the War of 1812; returned things to prewar conditions
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: The peace deal that ended the War of 1812. Nobody won or lost territory — it was basically a tie. But Americans felt like winners because they survived fighting the world's strongest military.
vocabulary.example: The Treaty of Ghent was basically a draw — no territory changed hands
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Treaty of Ghent was signed in 1814 to end the War of 1812 between the United States and Britain. Neither side gained or lost any territory, so the war ended in a draw. Despite this, many Americans felt like winners because they had stood up to the most powerful military in the world.

vocabulary.definition: The national anthem, written by Francis Scott Key after witnessing the battle at Fort McHenry
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Our national anthem! It started as a poem about watching the flag survive a brutal battle. The 'star-spangled banner' is the flag itself — covered in stars and still waving after the fight.
vocabulary.example: The Star-Spangled Banner became a symbol of American resilience and national pride
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Star-Spangled Banner is the national anthem of the United States, written by Francis Scott Key during the War of 1812. Key wrote the words after watching the British bombard Fort McHenry all night and seeing the American flag still waving at dawn. The song captures the pride and determination Americans felt during that era.

vocabulary.definition: A strong sense of pride and loyalty to one's country
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: That 'USA! USA!' feeling. After surviving the War of 1812, everyone was pumped up and proud to be American.
vocabulary.example: After the War of 1812, a surge of nationalism made Americans feel united and proud
vocabulary.typingSnippet: Nationalism is a strong feeling of pride and loyalty toward one's country. After the War of 1812, a wave of nationalism swept across the United States as Americans celebrated surviving a second war with Britain. This pride helped unite the country and fueled a period of growth and confidence.

vocabulary.definition: A period of national unity and one-party rule following the War of 1812 (roughly 1817-1825)
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: A time when everyone basically got along (at least on the surface). There was only one political party left, so there was less arguing. But problems were brewing underneath.
vocabulary.example: The Era of Good Feelings saw the Federalist Party collapse, leaving only the Democratic-Republicans
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Era of Good Feelings was a period of national unity from about 1817 to 1825 when the Federalist Party disappeared. With only one political party left, there was less conflict in government and Americans felt a shared sense of purpose. However, beneath the surface, tensions over slavery and states' rights were already building.

vocabulary.definition: An 1823 policy warning European nations against further colonization in the Americas
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: President Monroe basically told Europe: 'This side of the world is ours — stay out!' America couldn't really enforce it yet, but it was a bold statement.
vocabulary.example: The Monroe Doctrine told Europe to stay out of the Western Hemisphere, even though the US couldn't enforce it yet
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The Monroe Doctrine was an 1823 policy declaring that European nations must stop colonizing or interfering in the Americas. President Monroe warned that the U.S. would view any such action as a threat. Even though America was not yet powerful enough to enforce it, this bold statement shaped foreign policy for generations.

vocabulary.definition: Henry Clay's plan for economic growth: protective tariffs, a national bank, and internal improvements
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Henry Clay's three-part plan: tax foreign goods, create a national bank, and build roads and canals. The idea was to connect all parts of the country into one strong economy.
vocabulary.example: The American System aimed to connect the North, South, and West into one strong national economy
vocabulary.typingSnippet: The American System was Henry Clay's plan to strengthen the national economy after the War of 1812. It included protective tariffs, a national bank, and government-funded roads and canals. The goal was to connect the different regions of the country and make America less dependent on foreign goods.

vocabulary.definition: An 1819 Supreme Court case that upheld the national bank and strengthened federal power
vocabulary.simpleExplanation: Maryland tried to tax the national bank to shut it down. The Supreme Court said 'Nope — states can't mess with federal stuff.' This made the national government even more powerful.
vocabulary.example: McCulloch v. Maryland ruled that states could not tax federal institutions, reinforcing federal supremacy
vocabulary.typingSnippet: McCulloch v. Maryland was an 1819 Supreme Court case that ruled states could not tax the national bank. Chief Justice Marshall declared that federal law is supreme over state law. This decision strengthened the power of the federal government and confirmed that Congress had implied powers under the Constitution.

timelineEvents.title: Washington's Inauguration
timelineEvents.description: George Washington takes the oath of office as the first U.S. President

timelineEvents.title: Bill of Rights Ratified
timelineEvents.description: First ten amendments to the Constitution are officially adopted

timelineEvents.title: Whiskey Rebellion
timelineEvents.description: Farmers revolt against federal whiskey tax; Washington sends the army

timelineEvents.title: Jay's Treaty
timelineEvents.description: Agreement with Britain to remove forts, but limited American trade

timelineEvents.title: XYZ Affair
timelineEvents.description: French agents demand bribes from American diplomats, outraging the nation

timelineEvents.title: Election of 1800
timelineEvents.description: First peaceful transfer of power between political parties; Jefferson wins

timelineEvents.title: Marbury v. Madison
timelineEvents.description: Supreme Court establishes the power of judicial review

timelineEvents.title: Louisiana Purchase
timelineEvents.description: U.S. buys the Louisiana Territory from France, doubling the nation's size

timelineEvents.title: War of 1812 Begins
timelineEvents.description: U.S. declares war on Britain over impressment and frontier conflicts

timelineEvents.title: Monroe Doctrine
timelineEvents.description: President Monroe warns Europe against further colonization in the Americas

practiceQuestions.question: Washington's Cabinet included heads of which three departments?
options: State, Treasury, and War
Justice, Commerce, and Interior
Treasury, Defense, and Education
State, Labor, and Agriculture
practiceQuestions.explanation: Washington created the first Cabinet with three departments: State (led by Jefferson), Treasury (led by Hamilton), and War (led by Knox). These advisors helped the president make major decisions for the new nation.

practiceQuestions.question: A 'precedent' is best defined as:
options: A law passed by Congress
An action that becomes an example for the future
A decision made by the Supreme Court
A formal ceremony for a new president
practiceQuestions.explanation: A precedent is an action or decision that sets an example for future leaders. Washington set many precedents, like creating a Cabinet and serving only two terms, that future presidents followed.

practiceQuestions.question: Alexander Hamilton proposed a National Bank to:
options: Print currency and manage the nation's finances
Give loans to farmers
Fund military expeditions
Pay for roads and canals
practiceQuestions.explanation: Hamilton argued a National Bank was needed to print national currency, manage government debt, and expand capital in the growing economy. He used the 'Necessary and Proper' clause to justify it.

practiceQuestions.question: The Whiskey Rebellion was significant because it showed that:
options: Farmers could successfully resist the government
Taxes on whiskey were unconstitutional
The federal government could enforce its laws
Washington was an unpopular president
practiceQuestions.explanation: When frontier farmers in Pennsylvania revolted against the whiskey tax in 1794, Washington sent the army to crush the rebellion. This proved the new federal government had the power and will to enforce national laws.

practiceQuestions.question: Jay's Treaty was unpopular among Americans because:
options: It gave too much land to Britain
It didn't fully stop Britain from seizing American ships
It required Americans to pay tribute
It banned trade with France
practiceQuestions.explanation: Jay's Treaty (1794) got Britain to remove forts from American soil, but it didn't stop impressment or fully protect American trade. Only 2 of its main objectives were achieved, making it very unpopular.

practiceQuestions.question: The XYZ Affair involved:
options: British soldiers attacking American ports
Native Americans signing a peace treaty
Spanish explorers claiming American territory
French agents demanding bribes from American diplomats
practiceQuestions.explanation: In the XYZ Affair, French agents (called X, Y, and Z) demanded bribes before they would negotiate with American diplomats. This outraged Americans and led to an undeclared 'Quasi-War' with France.

practiceQuestions.question: Hamilton (Federalist) and Jefferson (Democratic-Republican) disagreed most about:
options: How much power the federal government should have
Whether to have a president
Whether to expand westward
Who should be vice president
practiceQuestions.explanation: The core disagreement was about federal power. Hamilton wanted a strong central government with implied powers (loose construction), while Jefferson wanted limited federal power with more authority to states (strict construction).

practiceQuestions.question: Which political party favored a strong central government and was led by Hamilton?
options: Democratic-Republicans
Whigs
Federalists
Anti-Federalists
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Federalist Party, led by Hamilton, favored a strong central government, a national bank, manufacturing, and a loose interpretation of the Constitution.

practiceQuestions.question: 'Strict construction' of the Constitution means:
options: The government can do anything that's not specifically banned
The government should only do what the Constitution specifically allows
The Constitution should be rewritten every 20 years
Only the Supreme Court can interpret the Constitution
practiceQuestions.explanation: Strict construction means the government can only exercise powers explicitly stated in the Constitution. Jefferson used this argument against the National Bank, saying the Constitution never mentions creating a bank.

practiceQuestions.question: Which group supported the Federalist Party?
options: Farmers and ordinary workers
Enslaved people
Frontier settlers
Businessmen and bankers
practiceQuestions.explanation: Federalists were supported by businessmen, bankers, and merchants — people who benefited from a strong national economy and trade. Democratic-Republicans were supported by farmers and ordinary workers.

practiceQuestions.question: The Election of 1800 was called the 'Revolution of 1800' because:
options: There was a violent uprising that overthrew the government
Thomas Jefferson refused to take office for several months
It eliminated the Electoral College from the Constitution
It was the first peaceful transfer of power between parties
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Election of 1800 was revolutionary because power transferred peacefully from the Federalist Party (Adams) to the Democratic-Republican Party (Jefferson). This proved American democracy could survive a change in leadership.

practiceQuestions.question: Marbury v. Madison (1803) established the principle of:
options: Judicial review
Executive privilege
States' rights
Separation of church and state
practiceQuestions.explanation: Chief Justice John Marshall's ruling in Marbury v. Madison established judicial review — the Supreme Court's power to declare laws unconstitutional. This gave the judiciary real power to check the other branches.

practiceQuestions.question: The Haitian Revolution influenced American history by:
options: Encouraging Americans to ban slavery immediately
Starting a war between the US and France
Causing Napoleon to sell the Louisiana Territory
Leading to the Missouri Compromise
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Haitian Revolution (a successful slave revolt) made Napoleon lose interest in his American empire. Without Haiti as a base, he decided to sell the Louisiana Territory to fund his European wars instead.

practiceQuestions.question: Jefferson faced a dilemma with the Louisiana Purchase because:
options: He did not want to add more land to the country
The Constitution didn't give the president power to buy territory
France refused to sell it at a price he could afford
Congress wanted to go to war with France over the land
practiceQuestions.explanation: Jefferson believed in strict construction — the government should only do what the Constitution specifically allows. But the Constitution never mentions buying land. He decided the deal was too good to pass up, using loose construction to justify it.

practiceQuestions.question: The Louisiana Purchase was significant because it:
options: Ended the conflict with Britain
Created a new branch of government
Freed all enslaved people in the territory
Doubled the size of the United States
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Louisiana Purchase (1803) bought the vast Louisiana Territory from France for $15 million, doubling the nation's size. This opened enormous western lands for exploration and settlement.

practiceQuestions.question: Sacagawea helped the Lewis and Clark expedition by:
options: Funding the expedition with her own money
Commanding the military escort
Serving as interpreter and guide through western territory
Drawing the official maps of the territory
practiceQuestions.explanation: Sacagawea, a Shoshone woman, helped the Corps of Discovery communicate with Native nations and navigate the western territory. Her presence also signaled to other Native peoples that the expedition was peaceful.

practiceQuestions.question: Impressment was the British practice of:
options: Forcing American sailors to serve in the Royal Navy
Taxing American goods at high rates
Blocking American ports
Printing false American currency
practiceQuestions.explanation: Impressment meant British naval ships would stop American vessels and force American sailors to serve in the Royal Navy. This was one of the main causes of the War of 1812.

practiceQuestions.question: The Embargo Act of 1807 backfired because it:
options: Caused Britain and France to declare war on the United States
Made the National Bank fail and collapse completely
Led directly to the signing of the Louisiana Purchase
Hurt American merchants and farmers more than foreign nations
practiceQuestions.explanation: Jefferson's Embargo Act banned ALL US trade with foreign nations to pressure Britain and France. But it devastated American businesses and farmers who depended on exports, making it extremely unpopular at home.

practiceQuestions.question: War Hawks were members of Congress who:
options: Opposed the War of 1812 and pushed for peaceful solutions
Pushed for war with Britain to defend honor and expand territory
Wanted to make lasting peace treaties with Native Americans
Supported the Embargo Act and wanted to expand trade further
practiceQuestions.explanation: War Hawks like Henry Clay and John C. Calhoun pushed for war with Britain in 1812. They wanted to defend American honor against impressment and expand into British Canada and Native American lands.

practiceQuestions.question: Tecumseh's confederation was an attempt to:
options: Help British traders in America
Create a new government in the West
Unite Native American nations to resist American expansion
Sign a peace treaty with Washington
practiceQuestions.explanation: Tecumseh, a Shawnee leader, tried to unite many Native American nations into a confederation to resist settlers taking their land. He allied with the British, but his confederation was weakened at the Battle of Tippecanoe.

practiceQuestions.question: The Battle of Fort McHenry is significant because:
options: It ended the War of 1812
The British captured the fort
It was the first battle of the war
It inspired Francis Scott Key to write 'The Star-Spangled Banner'
practiceQuestions.explanation: During the British bombardment of Fort McHenry in Baltimore (1814), Francis Scott Key watched from a ship and saw the American flag still flying at dawn. He wrote 'The Star-Spangled Banner,' which became the national anthem.

practiceQuestions.question: The Treaty of Ghent (1814) ended the War of 1812 by:
options: Returning everything to prewar conditions — basically a draw
Giving Britain control of Canada
Granting the US new territory
Requiring Britain to pay war reparations
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Treaty of Ghent was essentially a draw — no territory changed hands and neither side gained or lost land. Despite this, Americans celebrated the war as a success because they had defended their independence.

practiceQuestions.question: After the War of 1812, the Federalist Party collapsed because:
options: They had supported the war too strongly and lost credibility
They lost all their funding from wealthy northern merchants
They had opposed the war and were seen as unpatriotic
Their leaders moved to Britain and abandoned the party
practiceQuestions.explanation: The Federalist Party had opposed the War of 1812, and after the surge of nationalism following the war, they were seen as unpatriotic. The party collapsed, leaving only the Democratic-Republicans.

practiceQuestions.question: The Monroe Doctrine (1823) warned European nations to:
options: Stay out of American trade deals
Stop colonizing and interfering in the Western Hemisphere
Return land they had taken from Native Americans
Free all enslaved people in their colonies
practiceQuestions.explanation: President Monroe declared that the Americas were closed to further European colonization. While the US didn't have the military power to enforce it at the time, it became one of the most important principles in American foreign policy.

practiceQuestions.question: Henry Clay's American System included all of the following EXCEPT:
options: A national bank
Protective tariffs
Federal funding for internal improvements (roads, canals)
Free land for settlers
practiceQuestions.explanation: The American System had three parts: a new National Bank, protective tariffs to help American manufacturing, and federal funding for internal improvements like roads and canals. Free land for settlers was not part of Clay's plan.

practiceQuestions.question: McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) was important because it:
options: Established freedom of speech as a protected right for all citizens
Gave the president the power to declare war without Congress
Ruled that states could not tax federal institutions like the bank
Created the Electoral College system for choosing the president
practiceQuestions.explanation: When Maryland tried to tax the Second National Bank, the Supreme Court ruled states cannot tax federal institutions. This reinforced federal supremacy over state law and used the 'Necessary and Proper' clause to justify the bank.

practiceQuestions.question: The 'Era of Good Feelings' refers to a period of:
options: National unity and one-party rule after the War of 1812
War and conflict between the US and Britain
Economic depression and widespread poverty
Expansion of voting rights to all citizens
practiceQuestions.explanation: After the War of 1812, the Federalist Party collapsed, leaving the Democratic-Republicans as the only major party. This created a period of political unity called the 'Era of Good Feelings' (roughly 1817-1825).

practiceQuestions.question: Jefferson's Louisiana Purchase showed that even strong believers in strict construction:
options: Never change their minds
Always follow the Constitution exactly
Sometimes change when faced with real decisions and opportunities
Must get Supreme Court approval first
practiceQuestions.explanation: Jefferson had always argued for strict construction, but buying Louisiana required using loose construction (the Constitution doesn't mention buying land). This showed that real-world decisions can challenge even deeply held beliefs.

practiceQuestions.question: How does the debate between Hamilton and Jefferson connect to McCulloch v. Maryland?
options: They have no real connection to each other at all
Both involved whether the federal government could create a national bank
Both were about the causes and effects of the War of 1812
Both involved territorial expansion and westward settlement
practiceQuestions.explanation: Hamilton and Jefferson disagreed about whether the National Bank was constitutional (loose vs. strict construction). McCulloch v. Maryland (1819) revisited this same question and ruled the bank WAS constitutional, siding with Hamilton's view.

practiceQuestions.question: The essential question 'How did the first leaders shape the country we live in today?' is best answered by:
options: Their decisions about government power, parties, and expansion created lasting patterns
The first leaders had no lasting impact on the country we know today
Only Washington's decisions mattered because he was the first president
Everything they did was later reversed by the next generation of leaders
practiceQuestions.explanation: The first leaders' decisions — creating the Cabinet, establishing judicial review, forming political parties, expanding territory, and setting foreign policy principles like the Monroe Doctrine — created lasting patterns that still shape American government and politics today.

shortAnswerQuestions.question: Hamilton vs. Jefferson: Pick ONE of the major disagreements between Hamilton and Jefferson (national bank, strict vs. loose construction, economic vision, or foreign policy). Explain what each side believed and why it mattered for the future of the country.
rubric: Names a SPECIFIC disagreement (not just 'they disagreed')
Explains Hamilton's position with specific details
Explains Jefferson's position with specific details
Explains WHY this disagreement mattered for the future (3-4 sentences)
Uses vocabulary: strict construction, loose construction, political party
shortAnswerQuestions.exemplar: Hamilton and Jefferson disagreed about whether to create a National Bank. Hamilton believed in loose construction — that the 'Necessary and Proper' clause gave Congress implied powers to create a bank to manage the nation's finances, print currency, and help businesses grow. Jefferson believed in strict construction — the Constitution doesn't mention a bank, so the government has no right to create one. He feared a powerful bank would benefit wealthy northerners at the expense of farmers. This mattered because it created the debate about federal power vs. states' rights that continues today, and it led to the formation of America's first political parties.
sentenceStarters: Hamilton and Jefferson disagreed about...
Hamilton believed in loose construction, which means...
Jefferson argued for strict construction, meaning...
This disagreement mattered because it led to...

shortAnswerQuestions.question: Jefferson's Dilemma: Explain the dilemma Jefferson faced with the Louisiana Purchase. What did he believe about the Constitution? What did he actually do? Why does this matter?
rubric: Explains Jefferson's belief in strict construction
Identifies the specific problem: Constitution doesn't mention buying land
Explains what Jefferson decided to do and why (think about Napoleon's offer)
Uses the word 'dilemma' correctly
Explains what this teaches us about real-world decision making (4-5 sentences)
shortAnswerQuestions.exemplar: Jefferson faced a major dilemma with the Louisiana Purchase. He had always believed in strict construction — that the government should only do what the Constitution specifically allows. But Napoleon offered to sell the entire Louisiana Territory for $15 million, which would double the size of the nation. The problem was that the Constitution doesn't say anything about the president buying land from foreign countries. Jefferson decided the opportunity was too good to pass up and used loose construction to justify the purchase, even though it went against his own principles. This matters because it shows that even people with strong beliefs sometimes change when faced with real decisions — and that governing is more complicated than just following rules.
sentenceStarters: Jefferson believed in strict construction, which means...
The dilemma was that Napoleon offered...
Even though the Constitution doesn't mention..., Jefferson decided to...
This teaches us that leaders sometimes face...

shortAnswerQuestions.question: Escalation to War: Trace the path from peaceful disagreements to the War of 1812. What events led to war? Why did peaceful solutions fail? Use at least 3 specific events in your answer.
rubric: Names at least 3 specific events that led to war
Explains HOW each event contributed to growing tensions
Describes WHY peaceful solutions (like the Embargo Act) failed
Shows understanding of how things escalated over time
Uses vocabulary: impressment, War Hawks, Embargo Act (4-5 sentences)
shortAnswerQuestions.exemplar: Several events escalated tensions between the US and Britain before the War of 1812. First, Britain kept impressing American sailors — forcing them to serve in the Royal Navy against their will. Jefferson tried a peaceful solution with the Embargo Act of 1807, which banned all US trade with foreign nations, but this backfired and hurt American businesses more than Britain. Britain was also supplying weapons to Native Americans on the frontier, which angered settlers. Finally, War Hawks in Congress like Henry Clay pushed for war to defend American honor and expand territory. Peaceful solutions failed because Britain refused to stop impressment and the Embargo Act destroyed the American economy. By 1812, war seemed like the only option left.
sentenceStarters: Tensions between the US and Britain grew because of impressment, which means...
Jefferson tried to solve this peacefully with the Embargo Act, which...
The Embargo Act failed because...
War Hawks like Henry Clay pushed for war because...
By 1812, war seemed unavoidable because...

shortAnswerQuestions.question: Nationalism After 1812: How did the War of 1812 change how Americans felt about their country? Explain at least 3 specific ways nationalism grew after the war.
rubric: Defines or shows understanding of nationalism
Names at least 3 specific examples of growing nationalism
Connects the war to these changes (think: Era of Good Feelings, Star-Spangled Banner, American System, Monroe Doctrine)
Uses specific vocabulary terms from the unit
4-5 complete sentences
shortAnswerQuestions.exemplar: After the War of 1812, Americans felt a strong surge of nationalism — pride and loyalty to their country. First, the Federalist Party collapsed because they had opposed the war, leaving the Democratic-Republicans as the only party and creating the 'Era of Good Feelings.' Second, new national symbols emerged — the Star-Spangled Banner became a powerful anthem after Fort McHenry, and Americans celebrated the flag, the eagle, and shared national heroes. Third, Henry Clay proposed the American System to connect the economy with tariffs, a national bank, and roads and canals. Fourth, the Monroe Doctrine showed the world that America was confident enough to tell European nations to stay out of the Western Hemisphere. The war proved America could defend itself and made people think of themselves as Americans first.
sentenceStarters: Nationalism means...
After the War of 1812, the Era of Good Feelings began because...
One example of growing nationalism was the Star-Spangled Banner, which...
Henry Clay's American System showed nationalism by...
The Monroe Doctrine was an example of nationalism because...

shortAnswerQuestions.question: Connections Across the Unit: Pick ONE of the event pairings below and explain how they are connected. How does the earlier event help explain the later one?
connectionPairings: National Bank debate (1790s) → McCulloch v. Maryland (1819): How did Hamilton's argument about loose construction win out decades later?
Whiskey Rebellion (1794) → War of 1812: How did the government's willingness to use force set a precedent?
Alien & Sedition Acts (1798) → Monroe Doctrine (1823): How did early debates about government power shape how confident America became?
Jefferson's strict construction beliefs → Louisiana Purchase (1803): How did real-world events force Jefferson to act against his own principles?
rubric: Picks ONE of the suggested event pairings
Clearly explains what happened in BOTH events (use specific details)
Shows how the earlier event helped cause or explain the later one
Uses vocabulary terms that connect the two events
Demonstrates understanding of cause-and-effect across time (4-5 sentences)
shortAnswerQuestions.exemplar: The debate between Hamilton and Jefferson over the National Bank in the 1790s connects directly to the McCulloch v. Maryland case in 1819. Hamilton argued the bank was constitutional using the 'Necessary and Proper' clause (loose construction), while Jefferson said it wasn't mentioned in the Constitution (strict construction). Washington sided with Hamilton and created the bank. Decades later, when Maryland tried to tax the Second National Bank, the Supreme Court in McCulloch v. Maryland had to answer the same question — and they sided with Hamilton's view, ruling the bank was constitutional and states couldn't tax federal institutions. This shows how early disagreements about government power kept coming back throughout the Early Republic period.
sentenceStarters: The earlier event was..., which happened when...
The later event was..., which happened when...
These events are connected because...
The earlier event helped cause/explain the later one because...
This shows that in the Early Republic...

fillInBlankSentences.sentence: George Washington's ___ in 1789 set the example for how every future president would take the oath of office.
Washington appointed advisors to lead executive departments, forming the first presidential ___.
By serving only two terms, Washington set a ___ that future presidents followed for over 150 years.
Hamilton proposed a ___ to print currency, manage government debt, and strengthen the economy.
Hamilton wanted to place a ___ on imported goods to protect American manufacturing and raise revenue.
Washington sent the army to crush the ___, proving the federal government could enforce its laws.
Jefferson believed in ___, arguing that the government should only do what the Constitution specifically allows.
Hamilton used ___ to justify the National Bank, claiming the government had implied powers under the 'Necessary and Proper' clause.
In the 1803 case of ___, Chief Justice John Marshall established the Supreme Court's power of judicial review.
The ___ doubled the size of the United States when Jefferson bought the territory from France for $15 million.
___ helped Lewis and Clark communicate with Native nations and navigate the western territory during their expedition.
The British practice of ___ forced American sailors to serve in the Royal Navy against their will.
Jefferson's ___ banned all U.S. trade with foreign nations, but it hurt American businesses more than it hurt Britain or France.
After the War of 1812, a wave of ___ swept across the country as Americans felt united and proud of their nation.
The ___ of 1823 warned European nations to stop colonizing and interfering in the Western Hemisphere.

typingPassages.title: Building a New Government
typingPassages.source: Content adapted from The American Yawp (americanyawp.com) and Wikipedia for educational use.
typingPassages.passage: When George Washington took office in 1789, his inauguration marked the beginning of a brand new experiment in government. No one had ever been president before, so every decision Washington made set a precedent for future leaders. One of his first moves was to create a Cabinet of trusted advisors. He chose Thomas Jefferson to lead the Department of State, Alexander Hamilton to run the Department of Treasury, and Henry Knox to head the Department of War. Hamilton quickly became one of the most influential voices in Washington's government. He proposed creating a National Bank to manage the country's finances and print a national currency. He also pushed for tariffs on imported goods to protect American manufacturing and raise revenue for the government. Not everyone agreed with Hamilton's vision. Anti-Federalists worried that a strong central government would take power away from the states and threaten individual liberty. When farmers in western Pennsylvania refused to pay a new tax on whiskey, Washington faced his first major test. The Whiskey Rebellion showed that the federal government was willing to use military force to enforce its laws. Meanwhile, foreign crises tested the young nation. Jay's Treaty with Britain and the XYZ Affair with France forced Washington to navigate dangerous waters between two powerful European nations. Every choice he made shaped how future presidents would lead.

typingPassages.title: A Nation Divided by Ideas
typingPassages.source: Content adapted from The American Yawp (americanyawp.com) and Wikipedia for educational use.
typingPassages.passage: The biggest disagreement in early America was between Alexander Hamilton and Thomas Jefferson, and it split the country into two political parties. Hamilton led the Federalists, who believed in a strong federal government with the power to do whatever was necessary and proper to run the country. This idea was called loose construction of the Constitution. Jefferson led the Democratic-Republicans, who believed in strict construction. They argued the government should only do exactly what the Constitution says it can do, nothing more. The two sides disagreed about almost everything. Federalists wanted a national bank, protective tariffs, and close ties with Britain. Democratic-Republicans wanted an economy based on farming, friendship with France, and strong state governments. Federalists drew support from businessmen and bankers in northern cities, while Democratic-Republicans were backed by farmers and ordinary workers, especially in the South. The tension between these two parties came to a head in the Election of 1800. When Thomas Jefferson defeated John Adams, it marked the first time power had peacefully transferred from one political party to another. This peaceful transfer of power proved that American democracy could survive disagreement. People called it the Revolution of 1800 because the change happened through votes, not violence.

typingPassages.title: Doubling the Nation
typingPassages.source: Content adapted from The American Yawp (americanyawp.com) and Wikipedia for educational use.
typingPassages.passage: Thomas Jefferson believed in strict construction of the Constitution, but when France offered to sell the Louisiana Territory, he faced an impossible dilemma. The Constitution said nothing about buying land from foreign countries, yet the Louisiana Purchase would double the size of the United States for just fifteen million dollars. Jefferson decided the opportunity was too important to pass up and used loose construction to justify the deal. To explore this vast new territory, Jefferson sent Meriwether Lewis and William Clark on an expedition called the Corps of Discovery. With the help of Sacagawea, a Shoshone woman who served as their interpreter and guide, the expedition traveled all the way to the Pacific Ocean. They mapped new territory, made contact with dozens of Native American nations, and brought back valuable knowledge about western geography and resources. Meanwhile, Chief Justice John Marshall was shaping the Supreme Court into a powerful branch of government. In the landmark case Marbury v. Madison, Marshall established the principle of judicial review, giving the Court the power to strike down laws it deemed unconstitutional. As the nation grew, tensions with Britain escalated. The British practice of impressment, forcing American sailors into the Royal Navy, angered the young nation. Jefferson tried to solve the problem peacefully with the Embargo Act, which banned all American trade with foreign nations. But the embargo backfired badly, hurting American merchants and farmers far more than it hurt Britain or France.

typingPassages.title: Fighting for Respect
typingPassages.source: Content adapted from The American Yawp (americanyawp.com) and Wikipedia for educational use.
typingPassages.passage: By 1812, many Americans were fed up with British disrespect. War Hawks in Congress, led by Henry Clay, pushed hard for war with Britain. They wanted to defend American honor against impressment and dreamed of expanding into British Canada. On the frontier, the Shawnee leader Tecumseh was building a confederation of Native American nations to resist American settlement. When the War of 1812 began, America was not fully prepared. British forces even burned the capital city of Washington. But American resilience shone through at key moments. The defense of Fort McHenry in Baltimore inspired Francis Scott Key to write The Star Spangled Banner after watching the American flag survive a brutal overnight bombardment. The Treaty of Ghent ended the war in 1814, and while it was essentially a draw with no territory changing hands, Americans celebrated it as a great victory. A wave of nationalism swept across the country. The Federalist Party collapsed because they had opposed the war and were seen as unpatriotic, leaving the Democratic-Republicans as the only major party during the Era of Good Feelings. National pride fueled new ambitions. Henry Clay proposed the American System, a plan to unite the country through a national bank, protective tariffs, and federal funding for roads and canals. President Monroe issued the Monroe Doctrine, warning European nations to stay out of the Western Hemisphere. The Supreme Court continued to strengthen federal power, ruling in McCulloch v. Maryland that states could not tax federal institutions. America was no longer just surviving. It was growing into a confident nation.

quotes.text: The name of American, which belongs to you in your national capacity, must always exalt the just pride of patriotism.
quotes.author: George Washington

quotes.text: We are all republicans: we are all federalists.
quotes.author: Thomas Jefferson

quotes.text: A national debt, if it is not excessive, will be to us a national blessing.
quotes.author: Alexander Hamilton

quotes.text: Liberty, once lost, is lost forever.
quotes.author: John Adams

quotes.text: The accumulation of all powers, legislative, executive, and judiciary, in the same hands... may justly be pronounced the very definition of tyranny.
quotes.author: James Madison

quotes.text: A wise and frugal government... shall leave them otherwise free to regulate their own pursuits of industry and improvement.
quotes.author: Thomas Jefferson

quotes.text: The minority possess their equal rights, which equal law must protect, and to violate would be oppression.
quotes.author: Thomas Jefferson

quotes.text: There is not a man living who wishes more sincerely than I do, to see a plan adopted for the abolition of [slavery].
quotes.author: George Washington

quotes.text: The power of making war often prevents it, and in our case would give efficacy to our desire of peace.
quotes.author: Thomas Jefferson

quotes.text: The nation which indulges towards another an habitual hatred, or an habitual fondness, is in some degree a slave.
quotes.author: George Washington

quotes.text: Without liberty, law loses its nature and its name, and becomes oppression.
quotes.author: James Wilson
quotes.source: Of the Study of the Law in the United States, 1790

quotes.text: O say can you see, by the dawn's early light, what so proudly we hail'd at the twilight's last gleaming?
quotes.author: Francis Scott Key

quotes.text: Remember the Ladies, and be more generous and favourable to them than your ancestors.
quotes.author: Abigail Adams

quotes.text: If perticuliar care and attention is not paid to the Laidies we are determined to foment a Rebelion.
quotes.author: Abigail Adams

quotes.text: The origin of all power is in the people, and they have an incontestible right to check the creatures of their own creation.
quotes.author: Mercy Otis Warren

quotes.text: Our kind friend Mr. Carroll has come to hasten my departure, and is in a very bad humor... because I insist on waiting until the large picture of Gen. Washington is secured.
quotes.author: Dolley Madison
quotes.source: Letter to her sister Lucy, August 23, 1814

quotes.text: In overthrowing me, you have done no more than cut down the trunk of the tree of black liberty in Saint-Domingue. It will spring back from the roots, for they are numerous and deep.
quotes.author: Toussaint Louverture

quotes.text: I am of the African race. I have ever felt it my duty to promote the interest of my brethren.
quotes.author: Benjamin Banneker

quotes.text: Where today are the Pequot? Where are the Narragansett, the Mohican? They have vanished before the avarice and the oppression of the White Man.
quotes.author: Tecumseh

quotes.text: The Constitution of the United States was not made merely for the generation that then existed, but for posterity.
quotes.author: John Marshall

quotes.text: The bank is trying to kill me, but I will kill it.
quotes.author: Andrew Jackson
quotes.source: Regarding the Second Bank of the United States, 1832

funFacts: George Washington's teeth were NOT made of wood — they were made of hippo ivory, gold wire, and human teeth.
Thomas Jefferson and John Adams both died on July 4, 1826 — exactly 50 years after the Declaration of Independence.
Alexander Hamilton was the only Founding Father not born in the 13 colonies — he came from the Caribbean island of Nevis.
The White House was originally called the 'President's House.' It wasn't officially named the 'White House' until 1901.
During the War of 1812, First Lady Dolley Madison saved a portrait of George Washington from the burning White House.
The Louisiana Purchase cost about 4 cents per acre — roughly $15 million for 828,000 square miles.
John Adams was the first president to live in the White House, moving in while it was still unfinished in 1800.
Sacagawea carried her infant son on her back for the entire Lewis and Clark expedition — over 5,000 miles.
The Barbary Wars were America's first overseas military conflict — fought against pirates in North Africa.
George Washington refused to be called 'His Majesty' and chose the simple title 'Mr. President' instead.
Hamilton and Jefferson argued so fiercely in Washington's Cabinet that Washington compared them to 'quarrelsome neighbors.'
The original Star-Spangled Banner flag was 30 by 42 feet — about the size of a small house.
James Madison, the 'Father of the Constitution,' was only 5 feet 4 inches tall — the shortest president ever.
The Whiskey Rebellion was the first time the US government used military force against its own citizens.
Jefferson wrote to a friend that the Louisiana Purchase was 'the most important event since the birth of Christ.'
The Haitian Revolution (1791-1804) was the only successful large-scale slave revolt in history — and it helped make the Louisiana Purchase possible when Napoleon gave up on the Americas.
Toussaint Louverture taught himself to read at age 20 and went from being enslaved to governing all of Saint-Domingue, earning the nickname 'The Black Napoleon.'
Benjamin Banneker, a free Black man, wrote a letter to Thomas Jefferson challenging him to live up to the ideals of equality in the Declaration of Independence.
Tecumseh tried to unite Native American nations from the Great Lakes to the Gulf of Mexico into one confederation to resist American expansion.
Chief Justice John Marshall served on the Supreme Court for 34 years — the longest tenure ever — and established the principle of judicial review.
Andrew Jackson was the first president who wasn't from Virginia or Massachusetts, and he was the only president to have been a prisoner of war (captured at age 13 during the Revolution).
